title: Ambient Air Pollution Source Attribution And Health Impact In LMIC Megacities
summary: Build a verified workflow for attributing ambient PM2.5 to source sectors and estimating sector-specific health impacts in LMIC megacities, with explicit apportionment-methodology documentation, ground-monitor sparsity assessment, and the distinction between published and borrowed source-attribution data.
why_it_matters: Ambient air pollution causes an estimated 4.2 million premature deaths annually, concentrated in LMIC megacities. But dominant PM2.5 sources vary dramatically by city — household combustion contributes 20-60 percent in South Asian cities versus 5-15 percent in Sub-Saharan African cities where industrial and transport sources dominate. Source-apportionment studies are concentrated in high-income and Chinese cities — most LMIC megacities in South Asia, Sub-Saharan Africa, and the Middle East have few or no published studies. Without verified source-attribution data, air-quality policy in the most polluted cities is based on assumptions borrowed from different contexts.

success_metrics.target: Every source-attribution claim documents which apportionment method was used (chemical tracer, PMF, CTM) and how methodology choice affects attribution percentages.
success_metrics.validation_method: Schema validation and reviewer spot-checks against apportionment-methodology documentation.

success_metrics.target: Every city-level source-attribution claim documents ground-monitor density and whether the limited number of sites represents city-wide source contributions.
success_metrics.validation_method: Reviewer confirms monitor-density documentation and representativeness assessment for each analyzed city.

success_metrics.target: No source-attribution claim for an LMIC megacity without a published apportionment study is presented without explicit documentation that the attribution is borrowed from a different city context.
success_metrics.validation_method: Red-team reviewer confirms every borrowed-attribution claim has explicit transferability caveats.

review_policy.merge_gate: Schema validation, domain review, attribution-methodology safety review, and replication for quantitative outputs.
